title: Prayer of Saint Basil
note: Foundational prayer of St. Basil used as the opening scaffold for all canonical hours of the Saatat.
text:
O Master, Lord, God Almighty, Father of our Lord, God and Saviour Jesus Christ, we thank Thee for every condition, concerning every condition, and in every condition. For Thou hast sheltered us, helped us, guarded us, accepted us unto Thee, spared us, supported us, and brought us unto this hour.

For this cause we pray and entreat Thy Goodness, O Thou who lovest mankind, grant us to complete this holy day, and all the days of our life, in all peace with Thy fear.

All envy, all temptation, all the operation of Satan, the counsel of evil men and the rising up of enemies, hidden and open, take them away from us, and from all Thy people, and from this holy place that is Thine.

But those things which are good and profitable do Thou provide us: for it is Thine to have mercy and to save, O our God, and we send up glory to Thee, the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit, now and ever and unto the ages of ages. Amen.

title: Prayer of Thanksgiving
note: Standard introductory thanksgiving recited at the opening of each canonical hour.
text:
We thank Thee, O our Father, the Lord of all goodness and the Fountain of all blessing, that Thou hast caused us to pass this night in peace. O our Lord and our God, grant us also to pass this day in peace. Keep us from all sin, all wrath, and all temptation.

Grant that our prayer and our entreaty may rise up before Thee as incense, and that the lifting up of our hands may be as an evening sacrifice. Make us worthy to say before Thee at all times, without condemnation, in purity of heart: Our Father who art in Heaven.

For Thine is the kingdom and the power and the glory, of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit, now and ever and unto the ages of ages. Amen.

title: Anqaşa Birhān — The Gate of Light
note: Theotokos hymn appointed for the Tuat (Morning Watch) hour. 'Anqaşa Birhān' means 'Gate of Light', a title of the Virgin Mary as the one through whom the Light of the World entered the world. Sung or recited as the Marian element of the Tuat office.
text:
Hail to thee, O Gate of Light, through whom the Sun of Righteousness arose upon us.
Hail to thee, O Mary, the God-bearer, the pure dove, the spotless tabernacle.

Thou art the gate of the eastern dawn; through thee the Great Light came forth and dispelled the darkness of sin. Thou art the morning star that precedes the Sun: through thy intercession may we, who rise to pray in the watch of the night, be found worthy to behold the true Light.

Hail, O full of grace, the Lord is with thee. Blessed art thou among women, and blessed is the fruit of thy womb, Jesus Christ our Lord. Pray for us, O holy Theotokos, that we may be made worthy of the promises of Christ.

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit, now and ever and unto the ages of ages. Amen.

title: The Morning Watch (Tuat) — Saatat
note: The appointed prayer text for the Tuat canonical hour, corresponding to the night/early morning watch (6:00 AM). Drawn from the Ethiopian Saatat (Book of the Hours).
text:
At the morning watch we cry unto Thee, O Lord our God. For in the night-watches our soul has desired Thee; yea, with our spirit within us we will seek Thee early.

For the sake of Thy mercies, O Lord, we have waited for Thee. Our desire is toward Thee and toward the remembrance of Thy name. With my soul have I desired Thee in the night; yea, with my spirit within me will I seek Thee early.

Arising from sleep, we fall down before Thy goodness, O Master, and we cry unto Thee the Angelic Song: Holy, Holy, Holy art Thou, O God. Through the Theotokos have mercy on us.

Glory to God in the highest and on earth peace, good will among men. We praise Thee, we bless Thee, we worship Thee, we glorify Thee, we give thanks to Thee for Thy great glory.

O Lord, Heavenly King, God the Father Almighty; O Lord, the only-begotten Son, Jesus Christ; and the Holy Spirit: O Lord God, Lamb of God, Son of the Father, that takest away the sin of the world, have mercy on us, Thou that takest away the sins of the world. Receive our prayer, Thou that sittest at the right hand of the Father, and have mercy on us. For Thou only art holy, Thou only art Lord, Jesus Christ, to the glory of God the Father. Amen.

Every day will we bless Thee, and we will praise Thy name for ever and ever. Vouchsafe, O Lord, to keep us this day without sin. Blessed art Thou, O Lord God of our fathers, and praised and glorified is Thy name for ever. Amen.

title: The Third Hour (Meserkh) — Saatat
note: Commemorating the descent of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost.
text:
At the third hour we glorify Thee, O Lord our God, for at this hour Thou didst send down Thy Holy Spirit upon Thine apostles. Take not the Holy Spirit from us, O Good One, but renew Him within us who pray unto Thee.

O Holy Spirit, who didst descend upon the apostles at the third hour: take not Thy goodness from us, but renew us who pray unto Thee. Cleanse us from every defilement of flesh and spirit, and grant us to complete this day and all the days of our life in holiness.

At the third hour Thou wast led to the judgment hall, O Jesus our Lord, and Thou wast stripped of Thy garments and clothed in a robe of mockery. Yet Thou didst not turn Thy face from them that humbled Thee, but Thou didst endure all for our salvation.

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit. As it was in the beginning, is now, and ever shall be, world without end. Amen.

Lord have mercy. Lord have mercy. Lord have mercy.

O God, who at the third hour didst send Thy Holy Spirit upon Thine apostles: take Him not from us, O Good One, but renew Him within us who pray to Thee.

title: The Sixth Hour (Liku) — Saatat
note: Commemorating the Crucifixion of Christ.
text:
At the sixth hour we bow before Thee, O Christ our God, for at this hour Thou wast nailed to the Cross and didst take away the sin of the world.

O Lord our God, who at the sixth hour didst nail to the Cross the sin of the presumptuous Adam, and didst tear asunder the handwriting of our sins: O Christ our God, mortify also the lusts of the flesh, and save us.

At the sixth hour they stretched out Thy hands upon the Cross, O our Savior, and pierced Thy side with a spear. At the same hour grant us also to wound our bodies with the nails of Thy fear, and cause us to love Thy holy sufferings.

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit. As it was in the beginning, is now, and ever shall be, world without end. Amen.

Lord have mercy. Lord have mercy. Lord have mercy.

O Thou who at the sixth hour and on the sixth day didst nail to the Cross the presumptuous sin of Adam, tear asunder also the bond of our transgressions, O Christ our God, and save us.

title: The Ninth Hour (Serkh) — Saatat
note: Commemorating the death of Christ upon the Cross at the ninth hour. Drawn from the Ethiopian Saatat (Book of the Hours).
text:
At the ninth hour our Lord and God Jesus Christ breathed His last upon the Holy Cross, and gave up His spirit into the hands of His Father, and redeemed us from the curse of death.

O Christ our God, who at the ninth hour didst taste death in the flesh for our sake, and didst cry with a loud voice upon the Cross saying, 'My God, my God, why hast Thou forsaken me?' — forsake us not, O Lord, for Thou art our God and we have none other beside Thee.

Thou didst bow Thy head and give up the ghost; Thou didst rend the veil of the temple and open the tombs of the saints, and darkness covered the face of the earth. Yet through Thy death Thou hast trampled down death and hast given life to those in the tombs.

Have mercy on us, O Son of God, who was crucified for our sake. We bow before Thy Passion and Thy Holy Cross, and we glorify Thy Resurrection. For Thou art our God, and we know none other beside Thee, and we call upon Thy name.

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit. As it was in the beginning, is now, and ever shall be, world without end. Amen.

Lord have mercy. Lord have mercy. Lord have mercy.

O God, who at the ninth hour didst taste death in the flesh for our sake: mortify the presumptuous desires of our flesh, O Christ our God, and save us.

title: Vespers (Nimeat) — Saatat
note: The evening hour of the Saatat, corresponding to Vespers (18:00–21:00). Nimeat (ኒሜዓት) is the appointed hour of evening prayer in the Ethiopian Saatat.
text:
At the setting of the sun we glorify Thee, O Lord our God, who hast brought us through this day in Thy mercy. As the evening light fades, be Thou our light that never fails, for in Thee there is no darkness at all.

O Lord our God, as this day draws to its close, we offer Thee the evening sacrifice of prayer. We thank Thee for Thy mercies and Thy faithfulness which have followed us all the days of our life. Let our prayer be set before Thee as incense, and the lifting up of our hands as the evening sacrifice.

Grant us, O Lord, a peaceful evening and a sinless night. Clothe us with the armour of light as we enter the darkness, that we may sleep in Thy peace and rise to praise Thy name. May Thy holy angels surround us and keep us from all evil and from every assault of the adversary.

O Mary, the God-bearer, as the evening star appears, intercede for us before thy Son. May the same mercy that rested upon the disciples when Christ appeared to them on the evening of His Resurrection rest also upon us this night.

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit. As it was in the beginning, is now, and ever shall be, world without end. Amen.

Lord have mercy. Lord have mercy. Lord have mercy.

Vouchsafe, O Lord, to keep us this evening without sin. Blessed art Thou, O Lord God of our fathers, and praised and glorified is Thy name for ever. Amen.

title: Nimeat — The Seventh Hour (First Night Watch) — Saatat
note: The transitional watch between Vespers and the full Night Watch (21:00–00:00), recalling the completion of the day's labor and the beginning of the night vigil. This hour commemorates the descent of Christ's body from the Cross and His burial, as well as the prayer of the disciples in the upper room after the Resurrection. Authored Phase 2A.
text:
At the seventh hour we give thanks to Thee, O Lord our God, for the completion of this day's labor. As the light departs and the darkness of night descends, we place the work of our hands before Thy judgment seat, asking that what was done well may be accepted, and what was done in sin may be forgiven.

O Christ our God, at this hour Thy disciples gathered behind locked doors for fear, and yet Thou didst come and stand in the midst of them and say, Peace be unto you. Come also among us now, and speak Thy peace into the fears and the labors of this day.

O Lord, who receivedst the body of Thy Son from the tree of the Cross as the evening drew near: receive also the sacrifice of our prayer as an evening offering. Let Joseph and Nicodemus teach us how to come to Thee in the quiet hours, how to seek Thee when the crowd has departed and the noise of the day is stilled.

O Master and Lord of all, as the day's work is complete and we enter the first watch of the night, keep us from the temptations that come in the darkness. Let not the enemy find lodging in our hearts. Seal our senses with Thy grace, that eye and ear and tongue and hand may rest from sin and be renewed for Thy service at the morning watch.

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit. As it was in the beginning, is now, and ever shall be, world without end. Amen.

Lord have mercy. Lord have mercy. Lord have mercy.

O God, who didst receive into Thy keeping the soul of Thy Son at the close of the sixth day: receive into Thy keeping our souls at the close of this day, and grant us to rise at the morning watch to praise Thy name. For Thine is the kingdom and the power and the glory, of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit, now and ever and unto the ages of ages. Amen.

title: The Night Watch (Lelit) — Saatat
note: The night office of the Saatat (00:00–06:00), known as Lelit (ሌሊት). The night watch is the most ancient and honoured hour of prayer in the Ethiopian tradition, recalling the prayer of Christ in Gethsemane and the vigil of the Holy Women at the tomb.
text:
In the night watches we arise to praise Thee, O Lord, and to give thanks for Thy righteous judgements. As the stars keep their vigil above us, so do we keep our vigil before Thee, the Lord of the stars and of all creation.

O Christ our God, who didst arise from the dead in the watches of the night and didst destroy the dominion of death: arise in us also, and dispel the darkness of sin and ignorance from our hearts. As the holy women came to the tomb in the darkness before the dawn, grant us also to seek Thee with the same devotion and to find Thee risen and alive.

O Lord, who dost neither slumber nor sleep, and who keepest Israel: keep us also this night under the shadow of Thy wings. Let not our foot be moved; let not the sun smite us by day, nor the moon by night. Preserve our going out and our coming in, from this time forth and even for evermore.

We confess our sins before Thee, O Lord, committed this day in thought, word, and deed, and we ask Thy forgiveness. Wash us and we shall be whiter than snow. Let the night be a time of cleansing and renewal, that we may rise in the morning with clean hearts to serve Thee.

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit. As it was in the beginning, is now, and ever shall be, world without end. Amen.

Lord have mercy. Lord have mercy. Lord have mercy.

O God, who dost keep watch over all things and in whose sight the darkness is as the noonday: keep us this night in Thy peace, and grant us to rise and praise Thee at the morning watch. For Thine is the kingdom and the power and the glory, of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit, now and ever and unto the ages of ages. Amen.

title: Mazmur (Psalms) for the Tuat Hour
note: Appointed Psalms (Mazmur) for the Tuat canonical hour. The Ethiopian Saatat appoints Psalms 3, 63, and 133 for the Morning Watch.
text: The appointed Mazmur for the Tuat hour are Psalms 3, 63, and 133. These are resolved dynamically by the ETH_MAZMUR_SLOT handler from Scripture data.

title: Commemoration of Saints
note: Fallback commemoration rendered when no Senkessar entry is available for the current date, or when the Full (Metsihafe Sa'atat) edition is not active. In the Full edition, this component is superseded by eth-synaxarium-reading, which fetches the appointed narrative hagiography from data/synaxarium/ethiopian/. This component is retained for the Abbreviated (Horologium) edition and as a safety fallback.
text:
Let us commemorate the holy saints who have pleased God and who intercede for us before the throne of grace. May their prayers and their holy lives be an example and a strength to us.

May the prayers of the apostles, the martyrs, and all the saints be with us. Amen.

title: Senkessar — The Synaxarium Reading
note: Primary hagiographical reading for the Full (Metsihafe Sa'atat) edition. Appointed for the Lelit (Night Watch) and Tuat (Morning Watch) hours. The narrative text is fetched dynamically from data/synaxarium/ethiopian/ via the senkessar-index.json, keyed to the current Ethiopian calendar date. Falls back to eth-saints-commemoration if no entry is found.

resolution.note: renderOffice() must convert the current Gregorian date to its Ethiopian calendar equivalent before resolving this component. The Ethiopian calendar conversion logic should be added to calendar-engine.js in Phase 2.

text:
The appointed reading from the Senkessar (Book of the Saints) for this day.

This text is resolved dynamically from data/synaxarium/ethiopian/ and will be replaced at render time with the narrative hagiography for the current Ethiopian calendar date.

If no Senkessar entry is found for today, the general Commemoration of Saints will be offered in its place.

title: Leke Haile — The Doxology of Power
note: The doxology of power recited twelve times before the Mazmur in the Full (Metsihafe Sa'atat) edition of each canonical hour. 'Leke Haile' (ለከ ሃይለ) means 'Unto Thee the power' — the opening words of this ancient Ethiopian doxology.
text: To Thee be the power, the glory, the might, and the blessing, for ever and ever. Amen.

title: The Forty-One Kyrie
note: The litany of forty-one Lord have mercies recited in the Full (Metsihafe Sa'atat) edition.
text:
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

Lord have mercy.
Lord have mercy.

title: Weddase Maryam — Sunday (The Praises of Mary)
note: The appointed Praises of Mary for Sunday. This is the heart of the Sunday Sa'atat, composed of rhythmic, theological poetry celebrating the Incarnation.
text:
Nai'eet (Blessed art thou)... 

Blessed art thou among women, and blessed is the fruit of thy womb. Hail to thee, O full of grace, the Lord is with thee. Thou art the spiritual vessel in which the Manna was hidden; the Manna which came down from heaven, the bread of life which gives life to the world.

Thou art the golden lampstand which carried the bright Light at all times; the Light which is the Son of God, who came and dwelt in thee, and enlightened us who were sitting in darkness and the shadow of death.

Thou art the golden censer which carried the coal of fire; the fire which the Word of God took from thee, and through it He smelled the sweet savor of the world's salvation. Thou art the garden of delight, the garden of the spirit, in which the tree of life was planted; the tree which is Christ, our God and Savior.

O Mary, the Virgin, the Mother of God, the pure, the holy, the Mother of the Light: we magnify thee with the songs of the angels. Thou art higher than the Cherubim and more glorious than the Seraphim. Thou art the glory of the prophets and the pride of the apostles. Thou art the foundation of the martyrs and the strength of the righteous.

Intercede for us, O holy one, before thy Son, our Lord Jesus Christ, that He may forgive us our sins and have mercy upon us. Glory be to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit, now and ever, and unto the ages of ages. Amen.

title: Weddase Maryam — Monday (The Praises of Mary)
note: The appointed Praises of Mary for Monday. Monday's praise meditates on Mary as the Ark of the New Covenant and the dwelling place of the Most High.
text:
We praise thee, O Mary, the holy Virgin, the Ark of the New Covenant, in whom the Word of God took up His dwelling.

As the Ark of old was overlaid with gold within and without, so thou, O pure one, wast purified within and without by the Holy Spirit, that thou mightest be a worthy vessel for the Lord of glory.

As the manna was hidden within the Ark, so the true Bread of Life was hidden within thee — He who came down from heaven and gave life to the world. As the rod of Aaron budded and blossomed without the seed of man, so thou didst bear the flower of our salvation without the seed of man, by the power of the Most High.

Thou art the mercy seat upon which the presence of God rested; through thee the Lord of Hosts became man and dwelt among us. O holy Theotokos, thou art the meeting place of heaven and earth, the point at which the eternal entered time.

We bow before thy Son through thee. Intercede for us, O most holy one, that He who was pleased to dwell in thee may be pleased to dwell also in us by faith, and that we may be made worthy of His mercy both now and at the last day.

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit, now and ever, and unto the ages of ages. Amen.

title: Weddase Maryam — Tuesday (The Praises of Mary)
note: The appointed Praises of Mary for Tuesday. Tuesday's praise meditates on the Annunciation and the obedience of Mary.
text:
We praise thee, O Mary, who didst receive the message of the angel Gabriel with faith and humility, saying: Behold the handmaid of the Lord; be it unto me according to thy word.

In thee was fulfilled the word spoken by the prophet: Behold, a virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Emmanuel, God with us. The One whom the heavens cannot contain chose to be contained within thee, O blessed among women.

Thou didst not doubt the word of the angel, as did Zechariah; but thou didst believe, and therefore blessed art thou. Thou didst ask only how this thing should be, and the angel told thee: The Holy Spirit shall come upon thee, and the power of the Highest shall overshadow thee.

O Mary, thy obedience undid the knot that Eve's disobedience had tied. By thy fiat — Behold the handmaid — the salvation of the world was set in motion. Thy consent was the door through which the Savior entered the world.

Pray for us, O obedient one, that we also may say yes to God in all the moments of our lives, and that through our obedience His will may be done on earth as it is in heaven.

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit, now and ever, and unto the ages of ages. Amen.

title: Weddase Maryam — Wednesday (The Praises of Mary)
note: The appointed Praises of Mary for Wednesday. Wednesday's praise meditates on Mary at the foot of the Cross and her role as Mother of Sorrows.
text:
We praise thee, O Mary, who didst stand at the foot of the Cross of thy Son, and whose soul was pierced with a sword as Simeon had foretold.

O Mother of Sorrows, thou didst not flee when the disciples scattered; thou didst not deny Him when Peter denied; thou didst not betray Him as Judas betrayed. Thou didst stand, O steadfast one, and thy standing was itself a proclamation of faith.

Thou didst receive into thine arms the body of Him whom thou hadst carried in thine arms as a child. Twice thou didst hold Him: once in the joy of Bethlehem and once in the grief of Golgotha. Both times thou wast faithful.

O Mary, thou art the companion of all who grieve. Thou knowest the weight of sorrow and the length of the dark hours. Stand with us in our sufferings as thou didst stand at the Cross, and intercede for us before thy Son who endured all things for our sake.

Through thy tears and through His wounds, through thy sorrow and through His death, may we come to share in His Resurrection. For the night of weeping gives way to the morning of joy.

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit, now and ever, and unto the ages of ages. Amen.

title: Weddase Maryam — Thursday (The Praises of Mary)
note: The appointed Praises of Mary for Thursday. Thursday's praise meditates on the Visitation and Mary's role as bearer of Christ to the world.
text:
We praise thee, O Mary, who didst arise and go with haste to the hill country to visit thy kinswoman Elizabeth, carrying within thee the Lord of all.

At thy greeting, the babe leapt in Elizabeth's womb, and she was filled with the Holy Spirit and cried out: Blessed art thou among women, and blessed is the fruit of thy womb. How is it granted to me that the Mother of my Lord should come to me?

Thou art the first missionary, O Mary, for thou didst carry Christ to one who needed Him before He was yet born. Thou art the ark that moved, bearing the presence of God to the house of another. Wherever thou wentst, the Spirit went with thee, for thou wast full of grace.

Teach us also, O holy one, to carry Christ to those who need Him; to go with haste to those in need; to speak words that cause hearts to leap and souls to be filled with the Holy Spirit.

As John was sanctified in the womb at thy coming, so may we also be renewed and sanctified by the presence of thy Son, whom thou dost ever present to us in thy intercessions.

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit, now and ever, and unto the ages of ages. Amen.

title: Weddase Maryam — Friday (The Praises of Mary)
note: The appointed Praises of Mary for Friday. Friday's praise meditates on the Nativity and the humility of the Incarnation.
text:
We praise thee, O Mary, who didst bring forth in Bethlehem the Lord of heaven and earth, and didst lay Him in a manger because there was no room for Him in the inn.

O blessed poverty of that night! The King of kings was born without a palace; the Lord of hosts was wrapped in swaddling clothes; the One who holds all things in being was held in the arms of a young woman in a cave.

Thou, O Mary, wast the first to see His face; the first to hear His cry; the first to offer Him warmth against the cold of the world He had made. The angels sang in the sky above while thou didst sing softly to Him who is the song of all creation.

O Mary, thou art the Mother of God not by elevation but by humility. God chose the humble and the lowly, and thou wast the humblest of all. He that is mighty did great things for thee, and holy is His name.

Pray for us, O humble Mother, that we may receive Christ as thou didst — not with the pride of the inn that had no room, but with the openness of the manger, poor and bare and ready.

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit, now and ever, and unto the ages of ages. Amen.

title: Weddase Maryam — Saturday (The Praises of Mary)
note: The appointed Praises of Mary for Saturday. Saturday's praise meditates on Mary's vigil on Holy Saturday and her role as the keeper of faith in the silence between death and resurrection.
text:
We praise thee, O Mary, who kept the faith on the day of silence, when the disciples had scattered and the tomb was sealed and all hope seemed buried with thy Son.

On that great Sabbath thou didst not doubt; thou didst not despair. When the faith of the Twelve flickered, thine was the lamp that did not go out. Thou art the guardian of hope in the hour of desolation.

Thou art the Saturday of the soul — the one who waits between the death of what was and the rising of what shall be. In every darkness that seems final, thou art the witness that the stone is not the last word.

O Mary, be with us in our Holy Saturdays — in the days of waiting, of silence, of unanswered prayer, when heaven seems closed and God seems absent. Thou hast passed through that hour and kept thy faith; pray that we may keep ours.

As thou greeted the Risen Lord with joy in the garden of the Resurrection, so may we also come through our times of waiting to the joy of encounter with the Living Christ.

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit, now and ever, and unto the ages of ages. Amen.
